sections.opening: Sit, precious soul, the candle burns and the heart is open. Today we'll dive together into the depths of healing and protection psalms — Chapter 9, which seems like a 'small' psalm, but within it lies tremendous power for gratitude and children's healing. And in Chapter 11, we'll discover how to trust in God even when the whole world around us trembles. In the previous lesson, we learned about abundance and business success. Today we'll encounter the strongest passage in Psalms against fears — 'In the Lord I take refuge, how can you say to me: Flee like a bird to your mountain.' You'll discover how these words can completely transform your mood and inner confidence. The difference between someone who recites Psalms from memory and someone who knows the secret? This time you'll understand why every letter counts.
sections.theoryA:
How Does Chapter 9 Heal Sick Children?

Psalm 9 is called the 'children's healing psalm' for good reason. Its opening 'I will praise the Lord with all my heart' is the key that opens the gates of healing. When your baby is sick, Chapter 9 is the first spiritual address — not just because it's written in Kabbalah books, but because there's something deeper here.

The phrase 'with all my heart' says everything. True healing doesn't come from mechanical recitation, but from a full heart. Jewish mothers know this instinctively — they don't just say the words, they enter the prayer with their entire soul. This psalm is like a spiritual embrace that surrounds the sick child.

There's something else special here: the chapter speaks of 'giving thanks for miracles.' In other words, this isn't a prayer of request — it's a prayer of gratitude in advance. Faith that healing is already on its way. This changes the entire energy of the recitation. Instead of a cry of despair and fear, it becomes a celebration of faith.

And the technical details? Chapter 9 is recited while the child sleeps, gently, like a lullaby. There's a special virtue in placing your hand on the child while reciting — loving touch with holy words. Kabbalah says the child's soul is more 'open' than that of adults, so the holy words enter directly inside without obstacles.

practice1.instruction: Take Chapter 9 and read it aloud, with proper pronunciation. Imagine each word is a drop of light touching the soul. Notice how the word 'with all my heart' feels in your mouth — let it resonate. Don't rush, let each word do its work.

sections.theoryB:
Why Is Chapter 11 the Strongest Wall Against Fears?

Chapter 11 opens with the boldest words there are: 'In the Lord I take refuge, how can you say to me: Flee like a bird to your mountain.' This isn't just a beautiful expression — it's a declaration of war against fear. This 'how can you say' is a firm response to all the internal and external voices telling us to run.

There's a deep connection to astrology here — the bird mentioned symbolizes the air element, the thoughts that fly and confuse. But the psalm says: 'No, I don't fly. I stand firm.' This is the direct thread to the numerology of number 1 — independent standing, centrality, courage to decide for yourself.

The connection to Kabbalah is particularly interesting. The word 'I take refuge' connects to the sefirah of Chesed — the basic trust that the world is fundamentally good. When you enter Chapter 11, you activate this layer of consciousness. This isn't about naive optimism, but about deep faith based on connection to the Source.

The psalm continues: 'The Lord is in His holy temple, the Lord's throne is in heaven.' This isn't just beautiful poetry — it's a map of spiritual reality. There's an upper level (heaven) and an inner level (His holy temple). When you remember these two levels, the fears of the material plane become less significant. Not that they disappear, but there's a higher court.

practice2.instruction: Take the phrase 'In the Lord I take refuge' and repeat it 7 times in a row. Each time, imagine you're building a wall of light around yourself. Think about your biggest fear right now, and say to it in your mind: 'How do you tell me to flee?' Feel the power rising from within.

sections.deepDive:
What's Not Written in Books — The Secret of Psalms by Age

The hidden tradition of the Baal Shem Tov reveals something amazing: every person should say the chapter that corresponds to their age plus one. That is, a 25-year-old says Chapter 26, a 30-year-old says Chapter 31. Why?

Kabbalah explains that each year of life has a certain spiritual correction. When you're 25, your soul needs the energy of Chapter 26. This isn't random — it's based on the soul's spiritual journey throughout life. The plus one? That's the power that pushes forward, toward the next growth.

There's a hidden connection to numerology here. Every number in Psalms carries a certain energetic vibration. Someone who understands this can use Psalms like precise energy therapy. For example, someone born in the month of Adar (Pisces) would read Chapter 22 especially in Adar — that's the most suitable time for her.

And the greatest secret? When you say your age chapter every day for a whole year, it creates a kind of 'spiritual signature' in the soul. Like a seal sinking into soft wax. It becomes part of who you are, and you can access this power at any moment of need.

questions.question: Which chapter of Psalms is specifically designated for healing sick children?
questions.explanation: Chapter 9 is the psalm designated for healing sick children, with the opening 'I will praise the Lord with all my heart' and the virtue of giving thanks for miracles.

questions.question: According to the tradition of the Baal Shem Tov, which chapter should a 30-year-old say?
questions.explanation: According to the Baal Shem Tov's tradition, every person says the chapter corresponding to their age plus 1. So a 30-year-old would say Chapter 31.

questions.question: How does Chapter 11 begin?
options: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
In the Lord I take refuge
I will praise the Lord with all my heart
Why do you stand far off, O Lord
questions.explanation: Chapter 11 begins with the words 'In the Lord I take refuge, how can you say to me: Flee like a bird to your mountain' — a bold declaration against fear.

questions.question: Which Hebrew month is suitable for reading Chapter 22?
questions.explanation: According to the chart, the month of Adar (Pisces) is suitable for reading Chapter 22.

questions.question: Why do we add '+1' to the age when choosing a Psalms chapter?
options: Because it's the power that pushes toward the next growth
Because it fits the Hebrew year
questions.explanation: The plus one represents the spiritual power that pushes forward toward the next growth, according to the Baal Shem Tov's Kabbalah.

closing.summary: Today we discovered the special power of Chapter 9 for children's healing and the strength of Chapter 11 against fears. We learned about the hidden tradition of reading Psalms by age, and how each chapter carries precise spiritual energy.
closing.wisdom: In the Lord I take refuge — these aren't just words, it's a state of consciousness.
closing.nextPreview: In the next lesson we'll meet Psalm 23 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want' — the secret key to abundant livelihood. You'll discover why this is the most beloved chapter among successful business owners.

faq.question: How do we know which chapter to use for a specific problem?
faq.answer: Each chapter in Psalms has its special virtue. Chapter 9 for children's healing, Chapter 11 against fears, and Chapter 23 for livelihood. Additionally, there's always your personal age chapter.

faq.question: Should we read the age chapter every day?
faq.answer: Yes, according to the Baal Shem Tov's tradition, daily reading of the age chapter (plus 1) creates a 'spiritual signature' in the soul and strengthens inner power.

faq.question: Is it permitted to read Psalms at night?
faq.answer: Yes, especially healing and confidence chapters like Chapter 9 and Chapter 11. Sometimes night is the most suitable time because there are fewer distractions.

faq.question: How do we read Psalms correctly — by heart or from the book?
faq.answer: Both ways are good. From the book is more accurate, by heart is more from the heart. The main thing is intention and concentration, not whether you remember every word exactly.

seo.title: Chapters 9 & 11 in Psalms — Children's Healing and Protection from Fears
seo.description: Comprehensive guide to reading Psalms: Chapter 9 for healing sick children and Chapter 11 against fears. Includes the hidden tradition of reading chapters by age and connections to Kabbalah and numerology.
